sentences.text: Well, let's put it this way that the young people tried to still educate themselves, have education from people who had educations already who taught us.
And there were a lot of Jewish musicians.
There were opera singers, Jewish opera singers, and they still put performances together even though they were not supposed to.
Did it in hidden places, [BACKROUND NOISE] and they tried to, to make even in the ghetto a cultural life if possible.
Yes, I did.
I did.
I had a friend who was playing at the [INAUDIBLE], played the [INAUDIBLE], and he was in the orchestra.
And for rehearsals, I went with him in the basement somewhere, I remember.
I know a girl who's, who's in Florida.
She played Maria Stuart in and she was only 15 or 16 years old that time.
And she had a fantastic voice and was a very good actress at the time already.
She was from Berlin.
Yes, but I don't know if they want me to mention names.
I don't-- her name was Liana Peck.
Actually, there were two sisters, Liana and the other one was-- I forgot her name.
Both of them were playing in there, but Liana had the main lead.
There also was a-- from I believe there is a Mortimer in there, and the boy was from Hamburg.
And I believe he's a professor in California now with an [INAUDIBLE] already.
And they tried to make still culture in, in the ghetto.

And I feel that whatever they say about Theresienstadt, there was supposed to be, was supposed to be such a better ghetto than the other ones-- what made it better were the people in it, because they tried to keep dignity.
And it helped.
[SIGHS] Of course.
I believe it happened around the 3rd of October, 1944.
We got to Auschwitz in 19-- the 6th of October.
So it must have been like three, four days before that.
We had no idea.
Honestly, we had no idea.
They had told us that there are labor camps around the Leipzig-Dresden area, which is not that far from-- you know, on the German side of the border instead of the Czechoslovakian.
And we always heard about [GERMAN], which [GERMAN] happened to be a town in the-- is that [?
Saxony ?] up there?
I don't know.
And they always said that people from the ghetto going to [GERMAN].
For what reason, who brought it up, I have no idea.
So we thought we going either to another ghetto, or we have to work in [GERMAN] or whatever.
Landed up in Auschwitz.
I went with my mother and father together.
Before I answer that, my mother and father were in the transport.
I was not at that particular time, but I went anyway.
I just took my things, and I went to the--
What?
I was not-- I don't know if I wasn't supposed to go.
I was not in that particular transport.
I was not called in to go at that-- with that transport to Auschwitz.
But being that my mother and father were in there, I wanted to go with them.
I didn't know where I was going.
And luckily I did, because I think that had something to do that my mother and I got out together.
Saved our lives.
You were not allowed to do anything.
You just did.
Because it was all those people around there standing there with a little knapsack, not knowing what's happening.
There were thousands of people in those transports.
They didn't count you.
They didn't ask your name.
So I just went with them.
I wasn't asked a name or anything.
I just went on.
Well, first we had to-- like a-- again, [NON-ENGLISH] like a place, collecting place in the ghetto.
And from there they put us right there-- by then they had already the trains expanded from Auschwitz to Theresienstadt for the past year and a half before that.
And right from that [NON-ENGLISH] you went onto the cattle trains with the SS standing there and the dogs already and et cetera.
Cattle cars.
Yeah.
Full of people.
Some could sit down.
Some were standing up.
No air.
Nothing to eat.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the minute we were in the, in those cars, we knew this was it.
That was the last thing.
I believed that.
Yeah.
That-- I think-- and every one of us.
And, you know, the train ride from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz-- it's something nobody can explain.
You really can't.
No, I can't-- I mean, you can't explain it.
You can't.
It's impossible.
I think three days.
Something like three days and nights.
Something like that.
Couldn't get out.
You had no facilities.
You had-- the cars were closed.
No air coming in.
The guards, I believe, was on top of the cars, and then they had like those little wagons between the cattle cars where they were at.
All-- no.
Actually, were all my parents age and, and children and, and my age.
At that time, I was 17.
Nothing to eat.
Nothing to drink.
No place to relieve yourself.
I mean, it was like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dante's 'Inferno,' then already.
Just went.
I mean, it, it's--
It's-- it's--
--something when you think back over it, you can't really talk about it and explain, [BACKROUND NOISE] because it's the worst.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Not that I know of.
Nothing.
No.
Standing up for three days.
Because the wagons were so full of people that you couldn't.
The few people who went in first maybe sat down, and everybody was tumbling on top of each other.
[INAUDIBLE].
I don't know.
I really don't know.
I really don't know.
You heard a lot of crying.
You heard people talking to each other, whispering to each other, holding each other.
It was like something you cannot remember.
You, you don't want to remember.
I think that as bad as everything happened afterwards, I think that was the worst.
That was the worst.
I believe so, yes.
Yes.
Nothing.
The bodies were there, and when we got off the, the train, when they got us out of the train, they took the bodies out, and that was it.
I have no idea, any idea what happened afterwards to other people than to ourselves.
[SIGHS] Incredible.
It was terrible.
Terrible.
Terrible.
I don't know if you could get--
Of course it's offensive.
Of course you cannot get used to it, but you do not have a way out.
We were banging on the doors.
You know, they stopped the trains and [? said, ?] I guess, in the middle of the [INAUDIBLE] going up there on station.
We couldn't even look out.
Nobody opened up for us.
That was it.
No window.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing.
No, they're cattle cars.
You know, where they have the-- over the cattle cars.
Stopped--
--and you heard a lot of screaming from the SS, and a lot of [INAUDIBLE].
Then they opened up the thing, and [GERMAN], whatever.
Means like Jews should get out.
Of course, nobody else did anyway, right?
Jews out.
And we got out of there, and they put us in lines.
And then there was an SS man standing there with a revolver in his hand.
And he took my father on one side.
You know, he pointed from one side to the other.
Didn't talk.
He pointed.
Yeah.
With his hand and the revolver like that.
So over to the left was to the left-- little did we know what the left or the right was-- and over to the right was the right.
So my father went to the left.
My mother went to the left, and I went to the right.
And my mother got out of the line in front-- at that time we didn't know who it was, but it was Mengele.
And right in front of Mengele with the revolver in his hand started screaming at him.
Not he at her-- Your mother screamed at him?
My mother screaming at him.
You just took my husband away, who was fighting for you, for your fatherland.
I remember exactly the words.
I mean, I have to translate it in my head-- for your fatherland in 1914-18
My daughter, you don't take away from me.
Either she goes with me, or I go with her.
We didn't know what is what, right?
So he looked at my mother, and he said, how old are you?
And my mother said something like 45.
I think she was 46, 47, something.
So-- how old is your daughter?
So she made me automatically older, right?
She said something like 19, 20.
So he said, all right.
Go on that side.
So my mother got on the side where I was.
And that's how we would get through that we got into-- not knowing we would get through.
We didn't know what was happening to us.
But you were separated from your father?
Your father stayed--
We were separated from my father, yes.
My mother went, then, on my side, and we were-- were walked through a camp into some barracks, which turned out to be a sauna.
And--
A sauna.
You know, like what they call a sauna, like a cleansing place, whatever.
And that's where they shaved our heads and our body hair and took everything away from us.
And--
Mengele.
I don't know.
He must have been very surprised--
--that's for sure.
He must have been very surprised, but people were in such a daze what was going on.
Because we really didn't know what was going on.
I wasn't scared for my mother.
I was very scared for my father.
Because I didn't know when my mother went on the left side what would happen.
But then when they put my mother with me together, I said, all right.
At least we are together, but what's going to happen to Papa?
You know, and like that.
That was it.
He was 50 years old at the time. 50?
Yeah.
He had his 50th birthday still in the ghetto.
Yeah.
My mother-- I think I mentioned it to you before-- her very, very courageous woman.
Very.
And this is not the last we saw of Mengele.
We had an experience with him right before the war ended.
Well, we got-- well, we got into Auschwitz, as I told you.
And then we-- later on, we were selected to a labor camp.
And we landed up at [NON-ENGLISH], subsidiary of Gross-Rosen.
And-- which it was called Marzdorf.
And in March '45, Mengele must have gotten from-- Auschwitz was liberated in January, so he must have gotten before there to Gross-Rosen, not knowing to us.
We didn't know.
And we had a, a morning appell.
You know, like take the count, et cetera.
And what happened is here comes this SS man, which we didn't see before there with whole staff of people.
And my mother and I were like in the first row in the center.
And he stopped in front of my mother, and my mother had-- I mean, she looked bad already when we went to Auschwitz, but forget it.
After that, I mean, that was-- a little skinny, tiny little woman, you know?
No hair, et cetera.
And he stopped in front of my mother, and he looked at her.
He said, you only have to thank me that you are still alive, that you and your daughter are still alive.
So he recognized my mother and me after all that.
So which I, in my mind, believed that there were not many people who did that.
Must have left an impression somehow or the other.
Right?
Right.
Yes.
Were what they called-- actually, it was another selection, because there were some people who were taken away right at the time.
You know, we had to get completely undressed, get our hair shaven, et cetera.
Head shaven and body hair, et cetera.
And then there were some-- which I don't know if there were other SS doctors or whatever they were, but they were officers.
And they looked us up and down and took some of the people on the side.
And my mother was on, on that side again, on the opposite side.
Separated from me.
And I got out from my line and told my mother-- here we are, completely naked, no hair, no nothing.
I said, Mama, they won't recognize you anyway.
Go in the back of the line again.
And whatever happened, she got through that time.
She got-- you know, she got through on the line where I was.
The others were taken away.
So she snuck back into the line where I was, right?
But not behind-- directly behind me, on the end of the line.
And she got through.
So most likely, there was another SS doctor or another SS man or woman, whatever, and saw her and didn't recognize that she went through that line already.
I believe--
--that they went right into the gas, because there were people who had scars on their body.
And my mother had gone through when she was young a lot of surgeries.
And I guess that's what the, the reason that they put her on that side.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No, not old.
The people weren't old in that particular-- people we were with.
They were like maybe my mother's age.
Let's say between 45 and 50, which maybe sounded old for me then.
[LAUGHS]
I was 17.
I was 17 at the time.
Oh, yeah.
A lot of them.
And a few of us got through and, and made it alive and still meet as good friends here in the States.
The men were separated from the women, and--
Yes.
Razor-- you know, like the razors, like the old German type of razors they had.
Completely.
To the skin.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing.
All parts of your body.
Oh, of course.
Men, everybody was there.
That was the-- it was such a shock.
That you get like-- you're totally helpless.
You don't-- and still you didn't know what was ahead of you.
I don't think so.
The, the only thing I remember is some women said it's a good thing that their husbands don't see them that way.
That I remember how, how people acknowledge the fact what happened.
No.
No.
It was, like, mute.
Quiet.
Mute.
Nobody said a word.
You were like-- I can't explain.
It was almost like hypnosis, you know?
You had nothing left in you to, to live for.
And then they, then they gave us-- in another room, they gave us some clothes to wear.
Not our clothes, some clothes.
So I, I landed up with those-- you know, like they look like the Dutch wooden shoes?
The wooden shoes and a skirt and a jacket.
No, a dress and a jacket.
And the dress was all torn up and dirty, you know, and was open in front.
No underwear, OK?
And a jacket which was unbelievably nice.
[INAUDIBLE] I had it for one day.
A warm jacket on top of-- you know, it was October.
Could have used a, a warm jacket.
So here I was standing in the wooden shoes, in that dress.
I don't know how long it was.
I just know it was torn up to here.
You could see everything.
So I bit into the material to make a hole and ripped a piece out and bit holes in here to tie it together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK?
Then I had that nice jacket.
We get to the, to the barracks where we, you know, were put in, and they had the kapos, et cetera.
And I don't even think it took a day.
The kapo took my jacket.
So here I was without a jacket and a dress with holes.
[LAUGHS] And that's how I got out of Auschwitz.
Same type-- we all had the same type of-- like we would say in Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH].
And torn and dirty.
Rags.
No, not uniforms--
From other-- most likely from other prisoners.
The clothes, which was very old already or used by some and taken off when they were gassed.
Who knows?
No.
No, nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing at all.
How we looked?
All of a sudden, I felt we all look like alike.
Yeah.
We all looked alike.
I mean, I was-- when I got to, to Auschwitz into the sauna there-- what I called a sauna-- there were people who I knew.
Once the hair was off, you didn't know people anymore.
You didn't even-- maybe recognize them by their eyes, but that's all.
No.
Not that I remember.
No.
No.
Just the head and every other hair.
Yeah, underneath the arms and the rest of the body hair.
Yeah.
If there was, I, I didn't notice.
I'll be honest.
I don't know.
I really don't know.
I'm sure that was going on, but I don't know.
No.
At that particular--
--time, all you thought of, what's next?
The barracks were wooden barracks with a roof.
And you went in, and there was like in the middle of the barrack-- I believe they must have been horse barracks at one time, you know, like for horses and animals?
In the middle was like a walkway, a higher walkway.
That's where the SS come back and forth on.
On each side of them were what we called [? coverlets, ?] which-- the bunk beds, right?
There were five on top of each other.
Five levels?
Five levels.
No straw or, or covers or anything.
Just the wood and that's where [INAUDIBLE]-- on five at-- five people at a time on each.
No mattress.
No covers.
No nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Just us.
It was bitter cold.
It was very cold, and it was in Poland.
It's very cold there.
I don't know.
I don't know.
I have no idea.
I know being very cold and very hungry [BACKROUND NOISE] and very dirty [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and feeling my head.
Where's my hair?
[LAUGHS]
My mother and I, we were together all the time.
We huddled together.
Maybe that kept us warm.
Being afraid, because by then we knew when we saw the smoke from the chimneys that they were extermination gas ovens.
Being afraid from one minute to the other, now they take us.
Because it was like a period of [INAUDIBLE] waiting and doing nothing.
Once they either took us to what they called the latrine.
You know, to the--
--bathrooms.
And that's-- one day, a girl approached me-- if I have a piece of bread to give her, she would give me a knife.
And I still have the knife today.
And--
I gave her my bread.
And she gave me-- I wanted a knife.
Don't ask me why.
But I had a knife.
I was the big shot.
I had a knife.
Nothing to cut with, right?
And-- But if you were so hungry, why did you give up the bread?
I don't know.
I don't know.
I thought maybe with a knife I could save my life.
I don't know.
Yeah, of course.
She was right next to me.
Everything happened together to us.
From that minute on, everything happened together.
Well, in the morning was appell, which took for hours, standing in the cold, freezing till the SS can count it and count it and count it again.
Just let us stand out there.
And the rest of the time you were in the barrack, wondering what the next hour brings.
You were looking ahead to get our soup once a day.
All, all, all that-- what we got.
And--
You used to have-- me, personally, I never talked about it.
I didn't even write about it when I wrote it.
We had a terrible experience.
We had a soup next to us in big bucket.
I don't even know how we ate it.
And really now, I don't remember all that.
I just know one thing.
It must have been the third or fourth day in Auschwitz.
There was a finger in there with a, with a ring.
I have never, ever talked about it to anybody.
A human finger with a ring in it.
I guess we ate the soup.
I don't know.
But I remember that that was-- we were-- the five girls from [INAUDIBLE] that we sleeping on the bunk together, we were like in, in shock for days.
I mean, you know, this is things which you really bury so deep down you don't even want to talk about it, but it happened.
That happened.
No.
We didn't know any names from anybody.
That didn't existed if you knew names.
We didn't know any names.
We had a Ukrainian kapo, which was very bad.
And we had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a Jewish kapo, I believe there.
We didn't really know what they-- I know one was Ukrainian, because she had a different-- they were in uniform, right?
They had the striped suits and the hats and everything on.
And the Ukrainian had a, a red triangle on, so we knew that she was a political or whatever.
Turned out she was Ukrainian.
But she was bad, really bad.
Screaming and hitting and pushing and, and-- I think she's the one who took my jacket anyway, so that was bad enough for me.
And the other one was a Jewish kapo, but I don't know if any of these people even survived, because they were there much longer than us and stayed much longer than us.
And I'm sure they were gassed anyway or went on a death march.
I don't remember.
It was so bad that I really, really don't remember.
My mother is another thing.
Coming back from the appell into the barrack, she got out of line, and she run to the electric wire, and she wanted to, wanted to kill herself.
And just before she got to it, I had pulled her back.
No.
No.
No.
I just saw her running towards that, and that was it.
No.
We never spoke about it, ever.
Not even here.
We never talked about that.
OK.
It's a long story.
[LAUGHS]
